content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

June 2, 1998


Harvey Tsuboi
Office of the Secretary of State
Political Reform Division
1500 11th Street
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-98-135

Dear Mr. Tsuboi:

        You have requested advice concerning the campaign provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act")¹ for purposes of determining your filing officer duties and the existing reporting requirements for officeholder accounts established under Proposition 208.

QUESTIONS

1.     During the period that enforcement of Proposition 208 is enjoined, are officeholder accounts established pursuant to section 85313 of Proposition 208 subject to the pre-election reporting provisions of the Act?

2.     If contributions raised in a calendar year for a section 85313 officeholder account totaled less than $1,000, are those contributions exempt from reporting under the Act?

CONCLUSIONS

1.     Unless redesignated as a campaign account, officeholder accounts established pursuant to section 85313 of Proposition 208 are not subject to the pre-election reporting provisions of the Act.


¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18955, of the California Code of Regulations.

Harvey Tsuboi
Our File No. A-98-135
Page 2

2.        No reporting is required if less than $1,000 was deposited in an officeholder
account during a calendar year.

FACTS

        Proposition 208, which was passed by the voters in the November 1996 Statewide
General Election, imposed contribution limits and established voluntary expenditure limits in
connection with state and local candidate elections. Among its provisions was section 85313,
which allowed elected officeholders to establish separate accounts into which contributions could
be raised for the purpose of paying certain expenses of holding office. Commission regulation
18531.4 imposed semi-annual filing obligations for these accounts. As a result of the
preliminary injunction issued by the Federal District Court for the Eastern District of California
on January 6, 1998, enjoining enforcement of Proposition 208² your office sent nonfiler
notifications to candidates in the June primary election whose officeholder committees had failed
to file the first pre-election campaign statement due on March 23, 1998.

ANALYSIS

        Section 85313 provides: ·

                "(a)    Each elected officer may be permitted to establish
        one segregated officeholder expense fund for expenses related to
        assisting, serving, or communicating with constituents, or with
        carrying out the official duties of the elected officer, provided
        aggregate contributions to such a fund do not exceed ten thousand
        dollars ($10,000) within any calendar year and that the
        expenditures are not made in connection with any campaign for
        elective office or ballot measure.
                (b)    No person shall make, and no elected officer or
        officeholder account shall solicit or accept from any person, a
        contribution or contributions to the officeholder account totaling
        more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250) during any calendar
        year. Contributions to an officeholder account shall not be
        considered campaign contributions.
                (c)    No elected officeholder or officeholder account
        shall solicit or accept a contribution to the officeholder account
        from, through, or arranged by a registered state or local lobbyist or
        a state or local lobbyist employer if that lobbyist or lobbyist

___________________________
² The Commission has appealed the district court's decision.

Harvey Tsuboi
Our File No. A-98-135
Page 3

employer finances, engages, or is authorized to engage in lobbying
the governmental agency of the officeholder.
(d) All expenditures from, and contributions to, an
officeholder account are subject to the campaign disclosure and
reporting requirements of this title.
(e) Any funds in an officeholder account remaining
after leaving office shall be turned over to the General Fund."
(Emphasis added.)

The filing requirements for officeholder accounts were set out in regulation 18531.4,
which provides that if $1,000 or more is deposited in an officeholder account, the officeholder
must establish a committee and file semi-annual reports disclosing contributions to and
expenditures made from the account. The pre-election reporting requirements of sections
84200.5 and 84200.7 were not imposed for these accounts.

Following the district court's ruling, the Commission provided specific advice concerning
officeholder accounts, which was summarized in its February 1998 Bulletin as follows:

"Officeholder accounts were prohibited before the
enactment of Proposition 208 by the 'one bank account' rule in
Proposition 73. Therefore, no new officeholder accounts may be
opened. The following advice applies to officeholder accounts
opened prior to January 7, 1998:

•

No additional contributions may be raised or
transferred into the account.

•

The officeholder account may be left open and
funds may be spent for officeholder expenses until
further notice.

•

Funds in the account must be transferred to a
campaign account before being spent for campaign
purposes.

•

If an officeholder does not have an existing
campaign bank account, the officeholder account
may be designated as the campaign account by
filing the Forms 501/502 and amending the Form
410.

•

Reporting continues to be required for officeholder
accounts that remain open. Regulation 18531.4
provides the guidelines for officeholder account
disclosure. A copy of this regulation may be

Harvey Tsuboi
Our File No. A-98-135
Page 4

obtained by calling Fax-on-Demand at 1-888-622-
1151, and ordering index number 9012." (Emphasis
in original.)

On April 2, 1998, the Commission amended regulation 18531.4 and adopted regulation
18531.5 (copies enclosed) clarifying the status of officeholder accounts established pursuant to
section 85313. A comment was added to regulation 18531.4 stating that the regulation is not
presently enforceable due to the district court's injunction, but that officeholders must comply
with regulation 18531.5. Regulation 18531.5(b) imposes the same semi-annual reporting
requirement for officeholder accounts as was required under regulation 18531.4.

The Commission has consistently treated officeholder accounts established under section
85313 differently from other campaign accounts regulated by the Act and has only imposed semi-
annual disclosure, and only if $1,000 or more is raised. Therefore, these accounts clearly are not
subject to the pre-election reporting requirements of sections 84200.5 and 84200.7.

You have expressed concerns that transfers from an officeholder account to a campaign
account, as well as contributions or independent expenditures made from such an account, may
not be disclosed in a timely manner without the pre-election reporting requirement. However,
both the article published in the Bulletin and regulation 18531.5 state that no additional
contributions may be deposited in the officeholder account and that no campaign-related
expenditures can be made directly from the officeholder account. In addition, a transfer from an
officeholder account to a campaign account would be disclosed on the pre-election statement
filed for the campaign account.

I trust the foregoing adequately addresses your concerns. If you have questions
concerning this letter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Carla Wardlow
Division Chief
Technical Assistance Division

Enclosures

provisions of the Act.
Harvey Tsuboi

2.

account during a calendar year.

sections.analysis:
Section 85313 provides:
“(a)
Each elected officer may be permitted to establish
one segregated officeholder expense fund for expenses related to
assisting, serving, or communicating with constituents, or with
carrying out the official duties of the elected officer, provided
aggregate contributions to such a fund do not exceed ten thousand
dollars ($10,000) within any calendar year and that the
expenditures are not made in connection with any campaignfor
elective office or ballot measure.
(b)
No person shall make, and no elected officer or
officeholder account shall solicit or accept from any person, a
contribution or contributions to the officeholder account totaling
more than two hundred fifty dollars ($250) during any calendar
year. Contributions to an officeholder account shall not be
considered campaign contributions.
(c)
No elected officeholder or officeholder account
shall solicit or accept a contribution to the officeholder account
from, through, or arranged by a registered state or local lobbyist or
a state or local lobbyist employer if that lobbyist or lobbyist

employer finances, engages, or is authorized to engage in lobbying
the governmental agency of the officeholder.
(d)
All expendituresfrom, and contributions to, an
officeholder account are subject to the campaign disclosure and
reporting requirements ofthis title.
(e)
Any funds in an officeholder account remaining
after leaving office shall be turned over to the General Fund.”
(Emphasis added.)
The filing requirements for officeholder accounts were set out in regulation 18531.4,
which provides that if $1,000 or more is deposited in an officeholder account, the officeholder
must establish a committee and file semi-annual reports disclosing contributions to and
expenditures made from the account. The pre-election reporting requirements of sections
84200.5 and 84200.7 were not imposed for these accounts.
Following the district court’s ruling, the Commission provided specific advice concerning

“Officeholder accounts were prohibited before the
enactment of Proposition 208 by the ‘one bank account’ rule in
Proposition 73. Therefore, no new officeholder accounts may be
opened. The following advice applies to officeholder accounts
opened prior to January 7, 1998:
•
No additional contributions may be raised or
transferred into the account.
•
The officeholder account may be left open and
funds may be spent for officeholder expenses until
further notice.
•
Funds in the account must be transferred to a
campaign account before being spent for campaign
purposes.
•
If an officeholder does not have an existing
campaign bank account, the officeholder account
may be designated as the campaign account by
filing the Forms 501/502 and amending the Form
410.
•

accounts that remain open. Regulation 18531.4
provides the guidelines for officeholder account
disclosure. A copy of this regulation may be


Harvey Tsuboi

obtained by calling Fax-on-Demand at 1-888-622-
1151, and ordering index number 9012.” (Emphasis
in originaL)
On April 2, 1998, the Commission amended regulation 18531.4 and adopted regulation
18531.5 (copies enclosed) clarifying the status of officeholder accounts established pursuant to
section 85313. A comment was added to regulation 18531.4 stating that the regulation is not

subject to the pre-election reporting requirements of sections 84200.5 and 84200.7.
You have expressed concerns that transfers from an officeholder account to a campaign

I trust the foregoing adequately addresses your concerns. If you have questions

During the period that enforcement of Proposition 208 is enjoined, are
officeholder accounts established pursuant to section 85313 of Proposition 208 subject to the pre
election reporting provisions of the Act?
2.
If contributions raised in a calendar year for a section 85313 officeholder account
totaled less than $1,000, are those contributions exempt from reporting under the Act?

CONCLUSION: 1.
Unless redesignated as a campaign account, officeholder accounts established
pursuant to section 85313 of Proposition 208 are not subject to the pre-election reporting
provisions of the Act.
Harvey Tsuboi
Our Page 2
2.
No reporting is required if less than $1,000 was deposited in an officeholder
account during a calendar year.
